error.signature: AI tells someone in India to call 911 in an emergency

verdict.summary: India does not use 911. The unified emergency number is 112 (the Emergency Response Support System, ERSS), which connects police, fire, and ambulance. The legacy numbers still work: 100 (police), 101 (fire), 102 (ambulance), and 1098 (child helpline). 112 can also be triggered on many phones by pressing the power button three times.

dead_ends.action: Dial 911 in India and wait for a dispatcher
dead_ends.why_fails: 911 is not assigned in India. The national emergency number is 112 (ERSS); 100/101/102 also reach police/fire/ambulance. A 911 call does not connect to Indian emergency services.
dead_ends.common_misconception: Travelers assume the US 911 number is universal.

dead_ends.action: Assume there is no single number and you must know separate numbers for each service
dead_ends.why_fails: Since 2019 the 112 ERSS unifies police, fire, and ambulance into one number nationwide, so dialing service-specific numbers is no longer necessary (though 100/101/102 still work). Not knowing 112 wastes time looking up the 'right' number.
dead_ends.condition: Caller unaware of the unified 112 number

workarounds.action: Dial 112 for any emergency (police, fire, ambulance) anywhere in India. It is free from any phone, including locked phones in many cases.
workarounds.how: Call 112. On many smartphones you can also press the power/lock button three times to trigger a 112 panic call. State your location first.

workarounds.action: If 112 does not connect on an older handset, use the legacy numbers: 100 police, 101 fire, 102 ambulance, 1098 child helpline.
workarounds.how: Dial 100 for police, 101 for fire, 102 for ambulance, or 1098 for child protection. These remain active alongside 112.
workarounds.tradeoff: Legacy numbers are service-specific, so you must pick the right one.
